title: Economic profile: Cornwall, Ontario - Canada.ca
content:
## Economic profile: Cornwall, Ontario
Cornwall Footnote 1 has a population of 47,845, consisting of 25% seniors (65 and older), 59% working-age adults (15 to 64) and 16% children (under 15).
The key sectors of the economy in Cornwall, which account for 52% of the jobs in the region, are
- health care and social assistance
There are 17,795 employed residents, of which 9.4% are immigrants. Footnote 2
The economy generates a total of $1.9 billion in household and business incomes, which amounts to $40,477 per resident.

### General age structure – 2021
% children (under 15)
% working-age adults (15 to 64)
% of seniors (65 and older)
In order to support local services such as health care and social programs and maintain a good standard of living for everyone in the region, it is essential to have more working-age adults than seniors. Cornwall has fewer working-age adults than seniors compared to the average in Ontario.
From 2011 to 2021, Cornwall experienced
- a 3.2% increase in population, compared to an 11% increase in the province
- a 26% increase in the population’s proportion of seniors, compared to a 27% increase in the province
- an 8.5% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, compared to a 4.1% decrease in the province

### Population structure – Changes from 2011 to 2021
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 3 the region could experience Footnote 4
- a 4.1% decrease in population
- a 5.2% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, which could limit the availability and quality of local services
- a further increase in the population’s proportion of seniors by 21%, potentially changing the demand for local services, including health care and long-term care

### No immigration – Possible changes from 2021 to 2031
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
The region relies heavily on working-age adults to keep local services running smoothly and to maintain a good standard of living and economic growth. Immigrants can help by filling job gaps and bringing new skills and ideas, which keeps the local economy strong.
From 2011 to 2021, Cornwall experienced
- a 2.6% decrease in workforce Footnote 5 size, compared to a 7.8% increase in the province
- a 4.7% increase in the number of immigrant workers, compared to a 9.5% increase in the province
- a total of 4,424 retirements, Footnote 6 which amounts to 22% of the 2021 workforce, compared to 16% in the province
- a change in the average income per resident from local jobs relative to the province, which is 28% lower than the average in Ontario in 2021

Wage income generated from jobs Footnote 7
By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 8 the region could experience Footnote 9
- a 36% decrease in workforce size
- a 31% decrease in the number of jobs
- an increase in the proportion of seniors in the population from 25% to 31%

## Supporting the community’s French-speaking population
Francophone immigration outside Quebec plays an important role in enhancing the vitality and economic development of Francophone minority communities across the country.
Cornwall is part of the Welcoming Francophone Communities initiative launched by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada. This initiative supports the economic and socio-cultural integration of French-speaking newcomers.
In total, 38% of Cornwall’s population speaks French, with 19% speaking French as a first language and 19% as a second language.
Unless otherwise noted, all statistics are from the 2011, 2016 or 2021 editions of the Statistics Canada Census, and all calculations are from the Canadian Centre for Economic Analysis.
